Report has it the girl came from a maguey plantation in the state of Hidalgo, however in the rush of Eisenstein's departure from the United States, much of the official record of the expedition was lost - the cast of the players has never been found.

title_tesim: Richard Morris and Wayne Morris in The Quarterback
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph:GETS BROTHER START - having achieved film fame himself, Wayne Morris now is encouraging his young brother, Richard, who has acting aspirations. The elder Morris now is starred in a twin role in Paramount's "The Quarterback," with Virginia Dale and Lillian Cornell. Richard, just out of high school, weighs as much as his brother--a good 200 pounds, and also likes all kinds of athletes. The two are great pals despite a five-year difference in ages. Acting is a new line in the Morris family, the boys' parents being non-professionals.

title_tesim: Irene Lentz and Ben Turpin in The Golden Age of Comedy
caption_tesim: "The Golden Age of Comedy" Starring Will Rogers - Carole Lombard - Laurel and Hardy - Jean Harlow - Harry Langdon - Ben TurpinProduced by Robert Youngson
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: The 1958 film, "The Golden Age of Comedy," chronicles the variety of comedic styles and talent at the height of Hollywood's silent era. The film was culled from 2,000 reels of Mack Sennett and Hal Roach short film comedies and divided into eight acts with a prologue and finale. This photograph was taken from a scene from the 1923 (released January 6, 1924) silent film, "Ten Dollars or Ten Days," starring Ben Turpin.

Irene, the prolific costume designer and wardrobe supervisor for United Artists and Columbia throughout the 1930's and 1940's, began her career as an actress in a few silent films, often credited as Irene Lentz.

title_tesim: Douglas Fairbanks Jr. and Rita Corday in The Exile
caption_tesim: [cont.] Realizing he needs a new hideout, Charles disguises himself in peasant garb and sets out. Seeing Katie poling her barge along the canal, he nimbly jumps from a bridge into the boat and goes with her.

Text from back of photograph: The Exile introduces Douglas Fairbanks Jr. as a producer-star, exactly 30 years (1917) after Doug Sr. began producing his own films. Produced by The Fairbanks Company, Inc., for Universal-International release, the film co-stars Doug Jr. with Maria Montez and introduces Paule Croset (pronounced Paul Crozay). Leading roles are played by Henry Daniell, Nigel Bruce and Robert Coote. Directed by Max Opuls, The Exile is a romantic action melodrama based on the historic escapades of Charles II of England. Full of sword-fighting, chases, battle scenes and love-making, it is the kind of film ideally suited to the Fairbankses[sic], father and son.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Diane Hart and Molly Urquhart in Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: A.19. Mae Thompson (Diane Hart), a chorus girl at the Mercury Theatre, Edinburgh, pauses with alarm as she picks up a bottle of milk from outside the door of her apartment. For there across the landing is a woman from the dressmakers (Molly Urquhart) who has come to collect the money owed by Mae's room-mate Janet Jone. The woman has evidentally been there all night. Mae backs quietly into the room again. A scene from Happy-Go-Lovely, the Associated British-Marcel Hellman Technicolor musical set against the background of the Edinburgh Festival. David Niven, Vara-Ellen and Cesar Romero are the stars. Bruce Humberstone directs.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen in England after shooting Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Hollywood dancing star Vera-Ellen took advantage of the first free day she had after the completion of Happy-Go-Lovely to sped a few pleasant hours beside the River Thames at Cookham, in Berkshire. The autumn sunshine flashes on her face as Vera-Ellen, seated on this old rustic bridge, waves to a friend. Vera-Ellen co-stars with David Niven and Cesar Romero in the Associated British-Marcel Hellman production in Technicolor Happy-Go-Lovely. Bruce Humberstone directs.

title_tesim: Rosalind Russell, Sid Caesar and Melvyn Douglas in The Guilt of Janet Ames
uniform_title_tesim: Guilt Of Janet Ames, The  (Motion picture)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: From the AFI listing: A May, 1946 Hollywood Reporter news item noted that producer Virginia Van Upp's husband, Ralph Nelson, also a producer, was set to work on the film, but his contribution to the released film has not been confirmed. Various news items in HR indicate that production on the film was interrupted many times throughout the summer and fall of 1946, and that Van Upp and Charles Vidor, the film's original director, were embroiled in a bitter dispute about the script. In April, 1946, Vidor, while working on the film "Gilda," sued Columbia for termination of his contract. The suit was part of a long-standing dispute with studio executives and personnel that spanned many years. Production on the film was suspended for approximately two weeks in mid-August, 1946, after, according to a HR news item, Van Upp collapsed on the set. Van Upp was removed from the picture, and a HR news item suggested that her troubles stemmed from player commitments that forced her to begin filming the picture before the script was completed. Helen Deutsch took over Van Upp's production chores and, during the two-week production shutdown, supervised a series of script revisions and additions. A September 18, 1946 HR news item, noted that Vidor then became involved in a major dispute with Deutsch, whose supervision he was reportedly refusing. On September 24, 1946, HR printed a statement issued by Columbia production chief Harry Cohn, in which Cohn stated "emphatically and clearly that Miss Helen Deutsch is the producer of the picture and that Mr. Charles Vidor is not." The statement also reveals that Deutsch had asked to be relieved of her duties, and that Cohn insisted that she remain on the film. Vidor's suit against Columbia went to trial in December, 1946, at which time, according to HR, Vidor testified that Cohn made derogatory remarks to him and vilified his wife, Doris Warner. According to a September, 1947 HR news item, Vidor lost his suit against Columbia, and Columbia then sued Vidor.

repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: The 1958 film, "The Golden Age of Comedy," chronicles the variety of comedic styles and talent at the height of Hollywood's silent era. The film was culled from 2,000 reels of Mack Sennett and Hal Roach short film comedies and divided into eight acts with a prologue and finale. This photograph was taken from a scene from the 1924 silent film, "The Luck o' the Foolish," starring Harry Langdon.

title_tesim: Lew Ayres and Robert Siodmak in a publicity still for The Dark Mirror
caption_tesim: (Text from back of photograph) They Deal in Psychology and SuspenseFour years ago, when Lew Ayres went into the service, Robert Siodmak was struggling to convince Hollywood that his direction had authority and distinction to rank with the best. Now, with Lew out of the service and cast in his first post-war role, the same Siodmak, who has proved his directorial talent with "The Spiral Staircase," "The Killers," etc., is Lew's director. They combine their talents in "The Dark Mirror," Universal-International production written and produced by Nunnally Johnson. Lew plays a psychiatrist-crime unraveller and is co-starred with Olivia De Havilland, who plays a dual role as twins, one a charming girl, the other a maniacal murder. Thomas Mitchell is co-starred.
